sentences.text: Yeah. - Please continue the  story.
I, I was looking for-- getting the Aryan papers for my father, and I contacted some people, and I was told it would take several days.
So I contacted a Polish girl that I knew that worked for the Polish police.
And she offered to hide my father in her apartment, which is above the Polish police station.
She felt they would look for him over there, which was true.
So I was able to keep my father about four days, you know, in her apartment above the police station, till I had the papers for him.
Then we-- I took him and my mother in a horse and wagon to the town of Sandomierz.
We, we-- and from there, my father took a train to Debica, where he was supposed to meet us the next day.
We felt it was not wise to travel all together.
The next day, my mother and I came to the Debica, and we met my father.
And we went to the village of Wielpole again, where at that time my grandparents, and my uncles and aunts were living with my grandparents and my little brother.
In that village, we stayed about three or four-- three weeks.
On one night, the village was surrounded by the SS.
Flood lights and loud speakers were set up.

An announcement was made that if the able-bodied men and young men will volunteer to go to this particular labor camp, the women and children and the elderly person will not be resettled, will be allowed to remain in town.
We, at this time, we were subject to resettlement.
All towns were being liquidated.
We knew the atrocities that were happening, and the, and the loss of life.
So we-- my-- no-- the family got together, my father, I, and my uncle.
We decided that we should volunteer.
And about 100 men, two truckloads were loaded up.
And we went to the labor camp.
When were leaving, the families were lined up on the streets saying goodbye.
And the last picture, the last vision that I have of my mother, is her standing there with her arms on the shoulders of my little brother.
My little brother crying, and my mother saying to me, saying goodbye to me, and telling me, I shall never see you again.
This is the end.
That was her premonition.
[COUGHS] We went to this place that-- called-- a labor camp, [NON-ENGLISH], which was a labor camp attached to an airplane factory, to airplane manufacturing, the Heinkelwerke.
About five weeks later, in July, the village was rounded up and all its inhabitants, contrary to the promises that the Germans made, were marched on foot to the town of Debica.
And the next day, put on the train and taken to the Belzec extermination camp. - Now this is your mother, your brother-- My mother-- - And many other relatives.
My brother, my grandparents, my uncles, my aunts, my cousins.
Total, about 56 members of my father's family in one day.
We-- I found out later on, about four weeks later, when they brought people from the town of Debica to our camp, and the conversation was a fellow who was a druggist.
He was telling me about this-- about a transport from Wielpole, having brought to Debica.
And before they were being put on the, on the, on the trains, on the cattle cars, this beautiful girl having come to him, to the, the drug store, and asking for quinine because she wanted to abort.
And as he was a describing her, I said, wait a minute.
I went to my uncle's locker and I took out a picture of my cousin, and I brought it to him.
And he says, yes.
That was her.
She was pregnant.
I never told my uncle.
I never told her husband, who was in the camp with me.
They were taken to Belzec, and as we later learned, at the time that they, they were given to live from the time they were-- that they came off the train to the gas chambers, about four hours.
So later on, I-- we surmised that they died on Thisa B'Av.
I verified it when I went to Washington to the Holocaust Museum, and I checked out the dates.
My, my father's family perished on Thisa B'Av, which is a day of mourning-- [COUGHS] For Jewish people.
The camp to which we came already had several hundred people in it.
Most of them came from the town of Mielec, and the town of Radomysl.

These were Jewish people who really didn't have any professions before that, but they all declared themselves that they were either machinist, or, or, or, or carpenters, or electricians in order to, to, to get a job that, that might save their lives.
What-- this was-- before the war, it was the center of Polish defense industry.
And now they were manufacturing parts for the Heinkel 111, also repairing other German planes that were being damaged during the German-Russian war.
Because the German-Russian war broke out in June of 1941, and this is a year later.
Upon arrival to the camp, to the camp, they tried to set up a, a, independent council, similar to what used to exist in Jewish towns, like a Kehilla.
So from each group, people gave representatives, you know, to be on this council.
The, the camp was run at, that time, by, by a Polish name-- by a Polish man of name of Falerowski, who, the first thing that he checked out were any valuables, when you came in.
They'd search you and try to-- took the valuables away.
The security of the, of the camp was the-- was the head of the security for the Heinkelwerke, a man by the name of Stein.
The camp was guarded by the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the industrial, industrial guards that, that lived outside the camp.

The work was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wasn't as difficult as, probably, as in other places, because those who were fortunate to work in the, in the, in the, in the inside the factory, and those with skills, those skills were needed.
I don't know, I haven't heard many people speak about it, it's a little known fact, but the Germans categorized, you know, the, the Jewish workers in three categories.
There was-- and we were wearing a, a, a sign was the letter R, which mean which meant [NON-ENGLISH], defense work.
There were also Z, which was civilian workers.
And A, for army.
You know, they work for the army.
And we were so-- probably, in the, in the papers, you know, that the Germans kept, all marked as [NON-ENGLISH], defense workers.
I worked on a, on a seven-foot long break that-- we bended metals.
I remember very distinctly, we used to bend parts for the [NON-ENGLISH], 50 transport planes, because they had corrugated bodies and they were shot up.
We had to make, you know, we had to make replacement parts.
The, the, the factory functioned six days a week, day and night.
The shifts were 12-hour shifts.
The-- the one shift was manned by the Jewish workers.
One shift was manned by the Polish worker.
You, you were given blueprints and a work order.
And if you were not skillful and by, by accident, you ruined the work or so on, your number was taken down.
And at an appropriate time, several days later, when there was enough people whose number was taken down, you were taken out and shot.
Similarly, the killing grounds were outside the camp.
They were called [NON-ENGLISH].
These were the killing grounds were they were bringing in Jewish people who were turned in by the Poles, from the foreigners.
They were brought in and they were executed, mostly by the gestapo from the town of Mielec.
The price, supposedly, for a Jewish head, was a pack of cigarettes or a pound of sugar.
And it must have been a scarce commodity, because there were many Jews who were being shot over there.
We knew when and how they were shot, because people from the camp had to go and dig their graves and bring their clothing [INAUDIBLE].
And they told us how they were shot.
They were undressed, made down to kneel over the grave, and shot in the back of the head.
And the reason we knew that was true, because the clothes that they brought, they were not stained with blood.
They were clean.
One day on my on my work detail, the Polish-- the man who manned the machine, the Polish worker, the civilian worker who used to come and didn't show up.
So I had to do his shift and then do my shift again.
So I was on my shift for 36 hours.
Towards the end of my, my 36-- the shift, I got very sleepy, very tired, couldn't keep my eyes up.
And I was afraid I was going to ruin the work.
And if I ruin work, you know, I was a dead man.
So I went, and I laid down to sleep.
I found the place between the black-out curtains, because the lights was always blacked out by, by, by long black curtains in the window.
And one of the guards, or the [NON-ENGLISH] that, that patrolled the premises, happened to have been one of the meanest guard.
He was Ukrainian.
His name was Podolnyi, saw my silhouette between the curtains and the window.
And he came into the shop, and pulled me out, and administered a severe beating, to the point that he broke the, the woodstock on his, you know, on his gun, on his carbine.
Finally, somebody went and ran to the German supervisor, who rescued me, pulled me out, because I was a valuable, you know, defense worker.
There was only two people who knew to operate the machine, and he pulled me out.
And I felt that I had to get out from this particular detail, because we had a very, very-- on the day shift where I worked.
Actually, on the night shift that I worked, because this happened, you know, at night.
The day shift was manned by the Polish worker.
We had a German [NON-ENGLISH] foreman, by the name of Schmidt, who was a very vicious individual.
And he, he used to hide the orders and the prints, and then used to beat you, and kick you, or hit you with a wooden mallet.
We had a couple boys who suffered from tuberculosis.
He put them on the detail in the milling machine, where they-- it was in several months they were finished because of the dust from aluminum.
And I felt I had to get away from the detail.
My father worked inside the camp, as a-- in the kitchen, because that was what he did during the World War I, worked as a cook in the army.
And he was able to, to get me a job on the outside in the projects where the German employees of the plant lived.
And what we used to do is do the garbage detail.
We used to clean the houses, whatever the German, occupants of these houses, needed.
We were chosen for this particular work detail.
Working over there, I found the most charitable woman, heavy-set woman.
She limped a little bit, and she used to come and bring food to us.
And she used to sneak-- you know, always some goods, some food.
And, and later on, she, she called me one time into her house and showed me pictures of her husband.
She was of Croatian descent.
And her husband was an engineer in the plant in charge of physical and chemical facilities, camp physical and chemical lab, all the testing, metal testing facilities.
His name was [?
Push. ?] And she said that she's going to speak to her husband, so he we'll require-- request my, my name to go and work for him, which did happen.
I got the job in the physical lab for testing metals.
This particular engineer, [?
Push, ?] was responsible for saving several Jews.
He had a young man by name of Ernst Noble working in his chemical lab.
And one time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the interns, German intern who was there for the summer, out of just spite or fun, shot Ernst Noble to the leg.
And normally, that meant execution, because you were not any more able to perform work.
Engineer [?
Push ?] made sure that Ernst Noble was not executed.
He was kept in a dispensary, and saved his life.
On another occasion, an elderly Jew by the name of Reichman, who did not survive, but his three sons survived and lived in, in New York after, after the war.
He worked in the facility where they had cyanide baths.
In order to form the aluminum for-- to shape it, you had to give it a bath in the hot cyanide bath, so it became pliable.
And one of these-- this particular Jewish man, he did something that ruined some material, and he was slated to be executed.
And General-- Engineer [?
Push ?] got on a bicycle, went to the killing grounds in [INAUDIBLE], and threatened the, the, the gestapo, that if they execute this particular man, he's going to shut down the department.
And he brought that man home, saved his life.
He, he nurtured myself and another fellow who worked in the x-ray.
He used to bring us food, and he used to bring us eggs, and we used to fry them.
And we used to fry them in the x-ray department.
We used to put the red lights on that we were, you know, was doing some work.
So one time, the inspection or big shots came in, and he wouldn't let them in.
And they insisted.
He says, OK.
If you go in, it's at you own risk.
If you become impotent afterwards, you know, be sterile, go ahead and go in.
And he wouldn't let them in.
And eventually they found out about his leanings.
He was anti-Nazi.
He was a, a socialist, and he did not like the Nazis.
And he did anything that he could, you know, to obstruct them.
They finally sent him out towards the end of 1943, to Rostock.
And somebody else took over the job.
After the war, after the war, my friend Ernst Noble, who was shot through the knee--
--Whom he saved, went to do business with General [?
Push ?] in Schweinfurt, Germany.
They opened the factory.
They, they manufactured tooth powder.
At that time, there was no material for toothpaste, but they manufactured tooth powder.
I really don't know what happened afterwards, but I know that, that Ernst Noble and he went into business together, and many people from the camp, you know, went to visit them.
Till, till the summer of 1944.
In 1943, the camp, the camps denomination was changed from [GERMAN] Arbeitslager, into Konzentrationslager.
And SS came and took over the camp.
And that point, they tattooed us, on the, on the forearm.
They tattooed the initials, KL, which stands for Kozentrationslager.
In German, the first letters of Kozentrationslager, which means concentration, are KL.

There, there was a book they just published about six months ago, under the name, It's about Hauptscharfuhrer Schwammberger who is the hauptscharfuhrer who was responsible, who was supervising, the tattooing.
Originally, he threatened to have the tattoo done on the forehead.
The KL was supposed to be tattooed on the forehead.
And he told us that unless we produced a certain amount of, of valuables, jewelry, and gold, this is what's going to be done.
So we, we collected-- the authorities in the camp collected whatever, whatever they could, but that wasn't enough for him.
So we were pulling out the gold from teeth of living people, and we gave him.
When he was caught in Argentina, extradited to Germany, he was caught with several bags of jewelry, and gold from teeth.
And I'm sure the gold for many of my friends' teeth was among the things that he was caught with.
Let, let, let me explain to you.
Up till that time, the supervision of the camp, the security of the camp, was maintained by the [NON-ENGLISH] and by the internal [NON-ENGLISH].
The Germans made sure that the Jews watched them-- watched each other, because they instituted what was known as group responsibility.
If a Jew ran away from a detail, work detail, 10 to 20 people from the work detail would be executed.
If you ran away from a barracks, from his barrack, the, the people who lived with him in that barrack.
If he ran away from the camp and he had family, there would be the first one.
So everybody was watching each other.
I'll tell you a little story.
And-- when the barracks were being built, a group of my friends and I, approximately the same age, we decided that we wanted to live in one room.
And we moved in the barrack, barrack number four.
And most of us were the same age.
We kept the room very clean, very neat.
The leader in the room was a fellow by name of Hamek Schuldkraut from Tarnow.
He was older than most of us.
He already has been married.
His-- he had a wife and child, who were killed during the creation of the Tarnow ghetto, before he was sent to us.
His wife refused to part with the child and they-- the child-- they shot the child in his wife's arms.
He was a little more hardened and experienced.
He was actually our, our, our leader.
He was very talented, very gifted.
He, he portrayed-- he-- with crayons he obtained in the plant, he portrayed the scenes from camp life, like issuing of food, you know, and lining up and [NON-ENGLISH] work.
And our, our room was, was the neatest one.
And whenever there was an inspection by SS or Red Cross, they brought them to our room, to show how, how, how well they treated the inmates.
We were young fellows.
We tried to. to, to keep ourselves neatly.
One time while he was working, he worked in the power [? press ?] department, somebody moved the mattress, and the power-- the, the, the the stem broke.
And his number was taken down.
We knew that he was going to be executed.
The, the night before his execution, we knew that this time came because the fellow who was the grave digger, [NON-ENGLISH], was called out to [NON-ENGLISH].
He dug several graves and came back, and told us that he dug graves for about 12 people.
So that particular night, we knew that, next morning, they are going to come and take him out.
That night, we all laid awake, at the beginning, to watch him, so he shouldn't run away, because we would be their next-- the logical victims.
And afterwards, we laid there struggling with our own conscience.
Finally, about-- in the morning, when the dawn was breaking, we said to him, Hamek, go run.
Run out.
Run away.
You know, there was, there was, there were means.
If you jumped off the barbed wire, it was not electrified barbed wire that was on the top of the thing.
You could run away.
You know, and run away.
He says-- we said, we don't care.
But he says, no I can't.
He says, I have no right to, you know, to endanger your life for my own safety.
And he was taken out-- it was the group of 12 other people-- taken to the killing grounds.
But he did not die-- --The way the others.
He refused to undress, and he just walked away.
And they shot him as he walked.
When they brought the clothes back to the camp, his clothes were the only one had blood stains on.
To us, he was always the hero.
The things that he taught us, that he said to us, were the things that, that, we used to repeat in our-- among ourselves, the things that gave us strength.
He was one of the brave.
In our, in our minds, in our perception, he was one of the brave people.
The-- as I told you, the administration of the camp was by, by the internal Jewish forces and by outside forces.

The Germans, whenever the gestapo from outside-- whenever the Germans suffered a, a, a, a defeat on the Russian front, whenever they got drunk, they would come to the camp and take the people out and shoot them.
In the camp, but take them outside and shoot them.
But otherwise, internal administration was in the hands of the Jewish, Jewish [NON-ENGLISH].
One time though, the Germans decided-- the gestapo sheriff came down, his henchman was Hoffman.
And they decided that besides inflicting physical damage, they are going to try to break our spirits.
And they came to the leadership of the camp and they requested 20 people for execution the next morning.
And they told them, unless you give us 20 people tomorrow, we'll come and we'll, we'll take you as first ones.
And we'll take every 10th person in the camp, and we are going to shoot them.
And there was-- they suffered some-- something terrible happened to them.
We were not aware of what was, what was the reason for it.
Well, a meeting-- I, I know the story well, because I was witness to what was happening.
A meeting was called for the eldest in the camp to decide whom you were going to turn in, whom you're going to give them.
Out of 2,000 some people, he had to give them 20 people.
And the people that were asked to come were, were people who in their life, at their hometowns, were prominent people, respected people.
We had some professors in the camp.
We had people from the [NON-ENGLISH], intellectuals.
And the discussion took place, and various people were proposed for execution.
Somebody suggested, let's take the sick ones.
They have no chance of survival.
Others suggested, let's take the troublemakers, the ones who always cause a problem.
Let's take ones who are thieves.
Let's-- different, different people were given, offered for different reason.
However, some people felt that this was too difficult a decision to be made, and some more people were asked to come into the deliberation.
And among them was Rabbi Schenken from Gorlitz.
And when the question was put to him, what to do?
He got up and spoke about the Jewish law, about halacha.
And he said that according to Jewish law, one man can not-- has no right to put another man, you know, in order to save himself.
And he quoted the, the dictum, how do you know your blood is redder than the other person?
They started to laugh at him.
I said, what are you talking about?
This is 1943.
We already knew about the Warsaw ghetto uprising.
We were-- we were already-- at that time, we knew-- we suspected our loved ones were gone.
We ourselves, we were already dehumanized.
We already-- we changed from, from, from decent people into, into animalistic behavior.
The, the whole-- everything around us, you know, all the laws under which we used to live were upturned.
The things that were normal were abnormal, and the things that were abnormal were accepted.
You know, behavior that, that, before the war would not be tolerated was, was accepted as normal.
This is what happens to you under those conditions.
They, they laughed at him.
What god are you talking about after what we have experienced?
He was very persistent.
He was very eloquent.
And he was able to turn the tide.
And the decision was made.
Let the Germans come next day and take whoever they want.
We will not give our comrades into them.
And the next morning the gestapo came, and they were furious.
He was furious because we defied-- [CLEARS THROAT] We defied him.
We wouldn't break our spirits.
And they took over 40 people from our ranks.
They lined us up, and they picked out 40 people, and they executed.
The only thing this thing accomplished, that they never repeated this request.
Somehow, despite our laws, we felt fortified in our-- you know, we felt more resolute, and we felt better about ourselves.
In 1943, when the SS came, the, the--
--The administration of the camp fell directly under the SS, because they brought with them Ukrainian and Lithuanian guards.
And they were inside the camp, and they guarded the camp.
They were in charge of security.
This particularly-- do we have time?
I'll tell the story about [NON-ENGLISH].
But we don't have the time?
OK.
No.
We better continue this on the next tape.
OK
